messages: Hi — I'm putting together a short brief about recent changes in eating habits and could use your help.
The audience is a public health newsletter, so keep the language accessible and avoid jargon.
Please write a 150-word summary describing how adults' eating patterns have shifted over the past five years.
After that summary, list four clear, actionable recommendations aimed at community-level programs, each with a one-sentence rationale.
Also include three simple quantitative indicators showing approximate percent changes in key behaviors over the same period (just the figures, no citations).
Please note whether those shifts appear to be short-lived post-event effects or likely longer-term changes.
No academic citations are necessary, but if you rely on assumptions, state each assumption in one short sentence.
Keep the tone neutral and avoid medical advice—focus on behavior, access, and policy options.
Minimal formatting please so I can paste it directly into an email; a short header line is fine.
If any point is speculative, mark it as speculative rather than presenting it as definitive.
Quick background: at my local grocery the plant-based section has doubled in shelving in the last couple of seasons.
One coworker swears by meal kits; they say it stopped them from ordering takeout so often.
I tried intermittent fasting for a month and found I actually snacked more in the evening—personal data point.
At a farmers' market demo I attended, people asked more about plant proteins than about meat alternatives.
My teenage cousin now brings his own lunch to school because of online food influencers—surprising for his age.
I've seen lots of TikTok recipes that are less healthy but go viral because they're visually appealing.
Late-night delivery options seem to be everywhere now; I wonder if that's shifting dinner routines.
A friend signed up for a snack subscription box and is always trying novel packaged snacks these days.
Maybe later we can add a sidebar with campaign wording, but I don't need that in the main brief.
In a small workplace poll I once ran, nearly half said they skipped breakfast at least some days.
My aunt changed her soda habits after a health scare, which led our family to rethink sugary drinks.
There's a Saturday community garden that's really popular with younger residents—food access on a small scale.
I heard an economist on a podcast say food price swings are making people cook at home more often.
Last year's food festival seemed packed and everyone was sharing oversized dishes on social feeds.
A neighbor started balcony gardening and swears it changed how much fresh produce they eat.
I wonder if curated grocery stores that market 'healthy convenience' are nudging certain purchases.
Sometimes 'clean eating' labels just repackaged the same processed products with prettier packaging.
I've been choosing stores more for their vegetarian options—makes shopping faster.
We could look at adolescent trends or school-lunch policies someday, but not for this short piece.
Small aside: my dog tries to steal my sandwich every afternoon—household chaos.
Okay, I'm ready for that brief now—could you produce it based on what we discussed?
Please make sure the overview notes whether the shifts feel short-lived or more long-term.
Include the percentage-style metrics we mentioned earlier so readers can see direction and approximate magnitude.
When you use assumptions, keep each to one short sentence so the reader knows when something is an estimate.
Keep each recommendation very concise with a single-line justification.
No need for citations—plain email-ready text is my preference.
Also avoid clinical guidance; recommendations should be about community programming, access, or messaging.
If a point is speculative, flag it clearly with a short note in parentheses or similar.
Format it as simple plain text with a one-line header, then the overview, then the recommendations, then the metrics.
Go ahead and generate the piece now.
